item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
being a little more precise

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:VERNON_WELLS.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy these kind of articles from time to time, i think it's nice to have these kind of stuff from time to time to have some sort of break from the more in-depth and more ocmplex articles in here, and it's a very neat concept that got some chuckles out of my mouth. The interview was a bit odd-sounding, and the logs were slightly weird, but i overly enjoyed the read, and i personally kept thinking of a certain character in an anime i like that can do this sort of thing.

Nice article, a solid +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As do I. :)

With the presence of actual thorough testing procedures, I was disappointed by two things:
1) They test with a softball, but they take it on faith that it does anything weird with baseballs?
2) The description says "any living organism", but it didn't explode the fruit.

But yes, I absolutely love the interview and the testing. Thank you for blessing us with this Bat What Makes You Explode.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, and I don't mind a little humor during it, but the last test went too long. I'm particularly not into death-jokes, so it was both tone-breaking and unfun for me.

By the way, why the researcher was setting the bat himself? What breake it the most for me was this, actually. A D-Class in this situation wouldn't be exactly fun, but at least would be believable (accidents happen).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would love to see what happens when someone uses this against 682.
+1

item.title: +100 Woo
item.content:encoded:
The good news is our test cow wasn’t killed when we hit it with a regular bat, so we’re at least seeing a notable difference now. -Dr. Towns

Christ my sides man. I wasn't expecting a line like that at all.

Gratz on the +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm like two months late reading this, but I actually remember seeing this while looking at kaktus' sandbox a while back. Glad it's up. +1

ness backthrow skip next plox

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been a while since I laughed out loud at an SCP. "Huh" really got me. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess is, that it would make up for the distance between its parts by turning into a very aggressive type of candy cotton, in the shape of a lizard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn. I like the extreme simplicity of the actual object. I really like the comedy of the Foundation scraping the bottom of the barrel trying to work out the exact specs of an mind-numbingly simple item - even if it didn't actually make me laugh, it raised a wry smile and I appreciate the point that is being gone for. [Incidentally, you could say this rather neatly encapsulates a particular problem which SCPs often have - of distinguishing between what is objectively interesting to the Foundation and what is narratively interesting for the audience. On a narrative level, they should have stopped at 'bat what goes boom' and called it a day, but they keep going because of course they need an exhaustive understanding of its mechanism.]

But there's a few bits I really don't like, particularly the interview log. 'Oh shit, I guess not, huh?' is a good line but the rest is pretty awkward. The dialogue is cheesy and the guy keeps doing unprompted about-faces whenever anyone pushes him on anything, like the world's least competent kid's detective story villain. I can understand that this was going for comedy, but it felt like the sort of joke that needs competent comedic actors to be funny rather than awkward. The final log also goes a little too far into LolFoundation slapstick nonsense for my tastes, though I'd buy it if there was a note or something suggesting that the bat might have a probability-affecting or judgement-impairing effect, but they're not testing for it because they don't want anyone else exploding just now.

All in all, I'm going to have to downvote, though it's not by much.

Also, a real nitpick, but I'm pretty sure victims don't 'spontaneously' explode, because the explosions are obviously and directly caused by the bat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Literally unreadable!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "huh" was the only part of this I didn't like, actually.

Edit: Specifically, every other note sounds like something a comically overzealous scientist would write. "Huh" sounds like the opposite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this is intentional, but the numbering of the test logs is inconsistent. It goes .01, .02, .3, .4, .05, .06 etc.

Otherwise, hilarious SCP.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does dado have a relative in the sporting goods business?

The realization that the bat has other properties was very well depicted. The testing logs were progressively more outlandish, until the last one was almost -J territory. I could clearly imagine the SCP careering around the test chamber as bloody chunks filled the air.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn I've been starved for content like this. Good ol' "Here is a item that does a weird thing".
Brings me back to the good old days.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's the homerun bat in super smash bros

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Consider this me upvoting your comment, as this is the funniest thing I've read today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the ideal test log. You may not like it, but this is what peak scientific performance looks like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
honestly, this is good in a lot of ways. but the thing that made me upvote was this line:

Notes: Huh. -Dr. Towns

sometimes the simplest, goofiest shit is what elevates something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
our control chicken was also killed when we hit it with a non-anomalous baseball bat

Upvote sealed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Can you imagine this fucking test, though?
– hide block

Dr. Towns: D-7019, please enter the testing chamber, grasp the device, and apply it to the subject.

D-7019: You want me to hit a chicken with a baseball bat?

Dr. Towns: As hard as you can, please.

[D-7019 is silent for thirty-seven seconds.]

Dr. Towns: D-7019, please comply with—

D-7019: The fuck you want me to hit a chicken with a baseball bat for?

Dr. Towns: This is a control test.

D-7019: It's not gonna explode or set me on fire or anything, is it?

Dr. Towns: The items you are using are completely non-anomalous. The explosion already occurred in the experimental test.

D-7019: Well, when in Rome…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notably, our control chicken was also killed when we hit it with a non-anomalous baseball bat

The real D-Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All I could hear while reading the testing log was the Super Smash Bros. "PWIIIIING"

Nicely done sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Waiting eagerly for the 682 cross-test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There’s something strangely charming about the elements in play here. The interview with Mr. Thomas feels like a fresh of breath air on the wiki, and the simplicity of the skip greatly appeals to me. The testing logs are somewhat ridiculous, but I’m willing to overlook that. Great work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Towns: That’s all it does?

Mr. Thomas: (Pauses) Oh, shit. I guess not, huh?

I really liked this for some reason. And that last test log was hilarious. Like, "what if the Keystone Cops involved more spontaneous human combustion" hilarious. I really wasn't prepared for dark humor, coming into that, but have your +1, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m not a cop, and you won’t be getting a lawyer.

This was an unexpected little "Oh shit." moment I had while reading this. It's subtle, but it really nails the impression that in regards to the normal world, the Foundation is a giant in disguise, much bigger than regular people could ever imagine, and that what works or could potentially disrupt normal people and their normal affairs don't mean shit to the Foundation.

It's also a dawning moment for Thomas. He knows, or is at least tangentially aware, that his bat is abnormal and 'magical'. And now he knows that other people know, and they're probably not normal either. I can easily imagine Thomas' uncomfortable pause is him just going in his head, "Oh fuck, I might be in real trouble."

It's a fantastic line, and it really really goes to show that anything can work if it's well presented. It's just a bat that blows up living things with no explanation about its origins and a description two text blocks long. But even though it's a small gem, it's very well-cut. I thought the interview was perfect, the escalating test logs held my interest, and that last one, while goofy, was really unexpected and made me laugh.

d-(n_n)z +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gives me kind of a series 1 vibe, in a good way. It's a thing what does a thing, and I'd like th think there's always room for those if they're executed well. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember in chat like a year and a half ago djkaktus was talking about a bat that makes people explode. For some reason, that idea stuck with me enough for me to remember it.

Also, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah this is a little lolfoundation but I enjoy lolfoundation so here we are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a little on the ridiculous side but I did enjoy it a lot, so +1 from me.
Honestly though, I'm pretty shocked that this exact concept doesn't already exist somewhere on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. Very tentative upvote here. It's funny and well written, but something about the behaviour of the researchers seems to cross slightly into -J territory. The control chicken line got me chuckling, but then the end result just seemed… I dunno. Slightly unrealistic and forced, even for a bat that makes people explode.

Ah well, I laughed anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
K.O.

Here's something a little lighter that I've had in a sandbox for a while, that I think is kind of fun. Before anyone asks, yes, I know that's Vernon Wells, but something something names do not reflect actual people blah blah whatever.

Hope you enjoy it!

Image credit is from here.
